edges.description: JMJD inhibition alters histone methylation landscape
Epigenetic silencing impairs normal differentiation programs

nodes.description: 2-HG is structurally similar to alpha-ketoglutarate and competitively inhibits aKG-dependent dioxygenases, including TET2 (DNA demethylation), JMJD histone demethylases, and prolyl hydroxylases. This leads to widespread epigenetic and metabolic dysregulation.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations of isocitrate dehydrogenase 1 and 2 in intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma.
evidence.snippet: These mutations result in elevated levels of an oncometabolite, 2-hydroxyglutarate, which is associated with higher DNA CpG methylation and altered histone methylation that accompany a block in cellular differentiation.

nodes.description: Combined epigenetic alterations block normal cholangiocyte differentiation, maintaining cells in a progenitor-like state with proliferative capacity. This promotes tumor growth while the cells retain sensitivity to IDH inhibition, which can restore differentiation programs.

2-HG inhibition of TET2 blocks conversion of 5-methylcytosine to 5-hydroxymethylcytosine, causing DNA hypermethylation. This CpG island methylator phenotype (CIMP) silences tumor suppressor genes and differentiation programs, contributing to the block in cellular differentiation characteristic of IDH-mutant tumors.

2-HG inhibits Jumonji domain-containing histone demethylases, leading to accumulation of repressive histone methylation marks (H3K9me3, H3K27me3). This contributes to transcriptional silencing and the differentiation block.

IDH1 R132 or IDH2 R140/R172 mutations are neomorphic, conferring a new enzymatic function. Instead of converting isocitrate to alpha-ketoglutarate (aKG), mutant IDH converts aKG to the oncometabolite D-2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG). 2-HG accumulates to millimolar concentrations in tumor cells.

evidence.reference_title: Mutation profiling in cholangiocarcinoma: prognostic and therapeutic implications.
evidence.snippet: Commonly occurring GAs in intrahepatic CCA were TP53 (35%), KRAS (24%), ARID1A (20%), IDH1 (18%), MCL1 (16%) and PBRM1 (11%).
